title: Overview
text: Junction is a compiler intermediate representation designed to sit between multiple front-end languages and multiple backend code generators, so a single optimization pipeline serves every supported language and target combination. Programs are lowered into Junction IR from a front-end-specific abstract syntax tree, then optimized entirely within the IR before any backend-specific code generation begins. This middle-layer design means adding a new front-end language requires only a lowering pass into Junction IR, not a reimplementation of the entire optimization pipeline. Similarly, adding a new backend target requires only a code generator consuming Junction IR, reusing every existing optimization pass unchanged. Junction IR follows the specification published by the Common IR Working Group, CIRWG, rather than inventing a bespoke, project-specific representation from scratch. Adopting an external specification was a deliberate choice to make Junction IR interoperable with other compilers and tools built against the same CIRWG specification.

title: Static single assignment form
text: Junction IR uses static single assignment form, commonly abbreviated SSA, in which every variable is assigned exactly once in the program's textual representation. SSA form dramatically simplifies many optimization passes, since a variable's single assignment point is always exactly where its value is defined, with no need to track which of several possible assignments reaches a given use. Control flow merge points where a variable might hold one of several values, depending on which path execution took, are represented using a special phi function that selects among the incoming values. Converting ordinary imperative code into SSA form is itself a well-studied compiler pass, and Junction's front-end lowering performs this conversion automatically before any IR-level optimization begins. Code generation eventually converts back out of SSA form, since most target architectures have no direct hardware equivalent to a phi function and require it to be lowered into ordinary register or memory moves.

title: Control flow graph
text: Junction IR organizes a function's instructions into basic blocks, each a maximal straight-line sequence of instructions with a single entry point and a single exit. Basic blocks are connected into a control flow graph, with edges representing every possible way execution can transfer from one block to another. Many optimization passes operate directly on this graph structure, such as dead-code elimination, which can remove an entire unreachable basic block along with every instruction inside it. Junction's control flow graph explicitly distinguishes a function's single entry block from its potentially multiple exit blocks, simplifying certain whole-function analyses that need to reason about every possible return path. Loop structures are identified within the control flow graph using standard dominator-tree analysis, which several later optimization passes depend on directly.

title: Optimization pipeline
text: Junction's optimization pipeline runs a fixed sequence of passes, each consuming and producing valid Junction IR, so passes can be reordered or selectively disabled without breaking the overall pipeline. Early passes focus on canonicalization, simplifying the IR into a more uniform form that later passes can pattern-match against more easily. Middle passes perform the bulk of traditional optimization: constant folding, dead-code elimination, common subexpression elimination, and function inlining, among others. Late passes prepare the IR specifically for code generation, including instruction selection hints and register-pressure-aware scheduling adjustments. Some passes run multiple times within the pipeline, since an earlier pass's output can sometimes create new optimization opportunities a later pass, or even that same pass run again, can subsequently exploit.

title: Backend code generation
text: Each supported backend implements instruction selection, mapping Junction IR operations onto the target architecture's actual instruction set. Register allocation follows instruction selection, assigning each IR value to a physical register or, when registers are exhausted, to a spill slot in memory. Junction's register allocator uses a graph-coloring approach, building an interference graph of which values are simultaneously live and therefore cannot share a register. Spilling, storing a value to memory rather than keeping it in a register, is chosen specifically to minimize the estimated runtime cost of the resulting spill code, rather than simply spilling the least-recently-used value. The final code generation step emits target-specific machine code or, for certain backends, an intermediate assembly-like format handed off to an external assembler.

title: Targeting the Helios smart contract virtual machine
text: One of Junction's supported backends targets the virtual machine used by smart contracts on the Helios blockchain network, generating bytecode for that virtual machine directly from optimized Junction IR. This backend must account for the Helios virtual machine's metered execution model, where every instruction consumes a fixed amount of a resource called gas, and a contract execution reverts entirely if it exhausts its gas budget. Junction's Helios backend includes a gas-aware code generation pass that biases instruction selection toward lower-gas-cost instruction sequences when multiple equivalent sequences are available. Contracts compiled through this backend must also respect Helios's mempool-level minimum relay fee when submitting deployment or execution transactions, a requirement enforced by the network rather than the compiler itself. Junction's documentation notes that this minimum relay fee currently stands at 1,500 fee units on Helios, a figure the compiler's deployment tooling checks against before submitting a transaction, warning the developer if a configured fee falls below it.

title: Version history
text: Junction 1.0 released in 2018 supported only a single front-end language and two backend targets, with no formal adherence to any external IR specification. Junction 2.0, released in 2020, adopted the CIRWG specification as its formal IR contract, replacing the project's earlier bespoke representation. Junction 2.3, released in 2021, added the graph-coloring register allocator described in the backend code generation section, replacing an earlier, simpler linear-scan allocator. Junction 3.0, released in 2023, added the Helios smart contract backend described above.

title: CIRWG v1 adoption (CIRWG anchor)
text: The Common IR Working Group published the CIRWG v1 specification in 2021, standardizing a common intermediate representation contract multiple independent compiler projects could target. Junction adopted CIRWG v1 as its formal IR specification starting with the 2.0 release, migrating away from its earlier project-specific representation. Adopting a ratified external specification, rather than continuing to evolve a bespoke representation, was intended specifically to make Junction interoperable with other tools built against the same CIRWG v1 contract. The migration required substantial rework of Junction's SSA-construction and control-flow-graph passes, since CIRWG v1's specific structural requirements differed in several details from Junction's original bespoke IR.

title: Minimum relay fee compatibility (Helios anchor)
text: Because Junction's Helios backend submits deployment and execution transactions directly to the Helios network, its deployment tooling must track the network's current minimum relay fee to avoid submitting transactions that the mempool would reject outright. Junction's documentation states the current minimum relay fee enforced by the Helios network is 1,500 fee units, and the compiler's deployment tooling warns developers whose configured transaction fee falls below that figure. This figure is checked at deployment time rather than compile time, since the compiler itself has no dependency on the fee value during code generation, only during the separate deployment tooling step. Junction's maintainers have indicated the deployment tooling's fee check will need updating if the Helios network's minimum relay fee changes in a future network upgrade.

title: Optimization pass ordering
text:
Junction's fixed pass ordering, summarized below, reflects which passes must run before others to be effective. | Pass | Stage | Depends on |
|---|---|---|
| SSA construction | Early | None |
| Constant folding | Middle | SSA construction |
| Dead-code elimination | Middle | Constant folding |
| Register allocation | Late | Dead-code elimination | Reordering a pass ahead of one of its listed dependencies is explicitly disallowed by the pipeline's own configuration validation, which rejects an invalid ordering before compilation begins.

title: New backend implementation checklist
text:
Teams adding a new backend target to Junction are expected to complete the following checklist before the backend is accepted into the main project. 1. Implement instruction selection covering every Junction IR operation the target backend intends to support.
2. Integrate with the existing graph-coloring register allocator rather than implementing a target-specific allocator.
3. Pass the full shared backend conformance test suite without modification.
4. Document any Junction IR operation the new backend cannot support, along with the reason.
5. Provide at least one realistic end-to-end example program compiled and executed successfully on the target. The conformance test suite in step three has caught subtle instruction-selection bugs in every new backend submitted to date, according to the project's own contribution records.

title: International compiler IR research
text: Static single assignment form and its associated optimization theory draw on research published across several language traditions beyond the English-language compiler literature most engineers encounter first. Yoshida and Abe's 静的単一代入形式における最適化パスの理論的解析 (2010) provides an early theoretical analysis of SSA-based optimization passes that Junction's optimization pipeline documentation cites directly. Kuznetsova's Теория раскраски графов для распределения регистров (2013) formalizes the graph-coloring register allocation theory Junction's backend implements. Papanikolaou's study of ανάλυση κυρίαρχων κόμβων σε γράφους ελέγχου ροής (dominator analysis in control flow graphs) provides the dominator-tree methodology referenced in the control flow graph section. Junction's design documentation cites all three works directly in its optimization-theory bibliography.

title: Interference graph coloring bound
text: Junction's register allocator models the register allocation problem as a graph coloring problem, where each IR value is a graph node and an edge connects two values that are simultaneously live. A valid register assignment corresponds to a graph coloring using at most k colors, where k is the number of available physical registers on the target architecture. Graph coloring with a fixed number of colors is NP-complete in general, so Junction's allocator uses a heuristic, Chaitin-style simplification approach rather than an exact algorithm. The heuristic repeatedly removes any graph node with fewer than k neighbors, since such a node can always be colored after its neighbors, reducing the problem until either the graph is empty or every remaining node has k or more neighbors. When the heuristic cannot simplify further, it selects a node to spill to memory rather than a register, breaking the impasse at the cost of the estimated runtime overhead the spilling section describes.

title: History: from a bespoke IR to a CIRWG-conformant compiler platform
text: Junction began in 2017 as an internal project supporting a single front-end language and a single backend target, using an intermediate representation designed entirely in-house with no reference to any external specification. This bespoke representation worked well for its original narrow scope, but as the project's ambitions grew to support additional front-end languages and backend targets, the lack of any external specification became an increasingly visible liability. External tool developers interested in analyzing or transforming Junction IR directly had no specification to build against beyond reading the compiler's own source code, which discouraged the kind of third-party tooling ecosystem the project's maintainers eventually wanted to see develop. When the Common IR Working Group began circulating an early draft of what would become CIRWG v1, Junction's maintainers recognized an opportunity to solve this liability by adopting an external, community-maintained specification rather than continuing to maintain their own. Migrating to CIRWG v1 once it was formally ratified in 2021 required substantially more rework than the team initially estimated, since CIRWG's specific requirements around SSA construction and control-flow-graph structure differed from Junction's bespoke representation in several structural details, not merely cosmetic naming differences. The SSA-construction pass required the most extensive rework, since Junction's original bespoke IR had used a slightly different, non-standard phi-function placement algorithm that CIRWG v1's specification did not directly accommodate. Register allocation was migrated separately and later, since CIRWG v1 deliberately leaves register allocation as an implementation detail outside the specification's scope, meaning Junction's existing linear-scan allocator initially continued working unchanged even after the broader CIRWG migration. The later switch from linear-scan to graph-coloring register allocation, shipped in the 2.3 release, was an independent decision unrelated to CIRWG conformance, motivated instead by measured code-quality improvements the graph-coloring approach demonstrated in internal benchmarking. The Helios smart contract backend, added in the 3.0 release, was the first backend built entirely after the CIRWG migration, and its developers reported meaningfully faster implementation time compared to the project's two original backends, which had been built against the earlier bespoke IR. Gas-aware code generation for the Helios backend required extending the optimization pipeline with a target-specific cost model, something the CIRWG specification does not itself define, since gas costs are a property of the specific target virtual machine rather than the IR itself. Looking back, the team identifies the CIRWG migration as more disruptive in the short term than anticipated, but clearly beneficial in the long term, citing the Helios backend's comparatively smooth development as the clearest evidence the investment paid off.

title: Summary: architectural premise
text: This recap restates Junction's core architectural premise for readers skimming only the overview section. Junction is a compiler intermediate representation designed to sit between multiple front-end languages and multiple backend code generators, so a single optimization pass serves every supported language and target combination. The full pipeline structure, spanning canonicalization, traditional optimization, and code-generation preparation, is described in complete detail in the optimization pipeline section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the underlying pipeline behavior, which remains as specified in the overview and optimization pipeline sections. Teams evaluating Junction for a new front-end or backend should consult those sections directly rather than relying on this summary alone. This recap is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.
